text: Hastelloy N is a trade name given the solid-solution-strengthened nickel-base alloy developed at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory specifically for use in molten fluoride salts up to $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (ref.
The alloy was originally designated INOR-8 and has a nominal composition of Ni-17% Mo-7% Cr-5% Fe.
This material is the primary metallic structural material in the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment (MSRE) which achieved criticality on June 1, 1965, at Oak Ridge, Tennessee.[2] This alloy has

The weldability of this material has received considerable attention in our program.<sup>3,4</sup> We found it necessary to establish a weldability test to determine whether specific heats of material could be joined satisfactorily.

During the past few years we have found that Hastelloy N as well as other nickel- and iron-base alloys are subject to a type of neutron irradiation damage that decreases the creep-rupture strength and fracture strain at elevated temperatures.[5,6] This damage is rather general for all austenitic iron- and nickel-base alloys and is attributed to the production of helium within the material due to the interaction of a neutron of thermal energy with $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ to produce $^{7}\mathrm{Li}$ and $^{4}\mathrm{He}$ (refs.
Several heats of Hastelloy N were used in this study and their chemical compositions and other pertinent details are given in Table 1.
Heats 65-552 and 2477 were vacuum melted; all others were air melted.
All base metal was annealed for 1 hr at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ prior to testing unless otherwise specified.
Seven weldments were involved in this study and the details are given in Table 2.
A standard welding procedure was used on all the welds, although some deviation was necessary in welds 7 and 8 because of the type of filler metal being used.
The tensile tests were run in an Instron Universal testing machine (10,000-lb capacity) at strain rates of 0.05 or $0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ .

We used a standard equilibrating time of $1/2 \, \text{hr}$ for each test prior to loading.
A core facility was used in the ETR where the thermal and fast (> 1 Mev) fluxes were each $3.2 \times 10^{14}$ neutrons $\mathrm{cm}^{-2} \sec^{-1}$ .
The fluence obtained there was $5 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ .
The ETR experiments were uninstrumented and the design temperatures were either less than $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ or $1112 \pm 180^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The temperature in this facility was $110^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The decrease in fracture strain above $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is due to
Ductility of Hastelloy N Welds and Base Metal in Tensile Tests at a Strain Rate of $0.05 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ .

This same observation was also made by Gilliland and Venard.<sup>3</sup> After a postweld anneal of 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the welded and base metal samples have very similar yield strengths.
Yield Strength of Hastelloy N Welds and Base Metal in Tensile Tests at a Strain Rate of $0.05 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ .
Thus, above $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the yield strengths of base metal and welds annealed for 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are equivalent and are unaffected by irradiation.
Figure 4 shows that the ultimate tensile strength is about the same for welded samples and for base metal.
This property is not affected significantly by postweld heat treatment at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Irradiation causes a slight (approx $10\%$ ) increase in the ultimate tensile strength at the lower test temperatures and a decrease at test temperatures of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and greater.
Ultimate Tensile Strength of Hastelloy N Welds and Base Metal in Tensile Tests at a Strain Rate of $0.05 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ .
Comparison of the Fracture Strains of Welds and Base Metal in Tensile Tests at a Strain Rate of $0.05 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ .
Welds 1, 4, and 5 involve air-melted materials with relatively high boron levels.
Heat 2477, a vacuum-melted heat containing 8 ppm B, was used as the base and filler metal in weld 3.
Weld 6 utilized filler metal from heat 65-552, a vacuum-melted heat containing 1 ppm B, and base metal from heat 5065, an air-melted heat.
Welds 7 and 8 utilized air-melted base and filler materials.

The filler rod for weld 7 was plasma-spray-coated with $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ in an effort to reduce the interdendritic spacing in the cast weld metal and to provide additional sites for helium collection.

However, most of the $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ floated on the weld and had to be removed mechanically before making the next pass; the aluminum content of the deposited weld metal was only $0.06\%$ compared with $0.84\%$ for a typical cross section from the filler metal before deposition.
Weld 8 involved a filler material coated with WC.
The deposited weld metal contained $2\%$ W and $0.1\%$ C, so the WC coating dissolved in the melt altered the properties of the weld appreciably.
1. base metal given a standard solution anneal of 1 hr at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ,
2. base metal given the 1 hr at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ treatment followed by an anneal of 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ,
4. transverse weld samples annealed for 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
At a test temperature of $75^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the following points are illustrated in Fig.
Figure 5 also illustrates several important points at a tensile test temperature of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A postweld heat treatment of 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ markedly improved the fracture strain, with values of 22 to $30\%$ being observed.
Postweld annealing for 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ had little effect on the postirradiation properties.
Welds 3 and 8 had properties superior to those of the other welds.
Irradiation reduced the fracture strain to about $2\%$ independent of whether the test sample was base metal or a transverse weld.
All of the postirradiation results presented thus far have been for materials irradiated at less than $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
We have irradiated several samples of these heats and others at 1200 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and find that there are at least two significant differences in the results obtained.
The displacement damage anneals at these irradiation temperatures and the tensile properties up to about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are the same for irradiated and unirradiated materials.
At higher test temperatures the fracture strain is reduced even further if the irradiation temperature is in the range of 1200 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

For example, heat 5065 was found to have a fracture strain of $11.3\%$ (irradiated at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ compared with the value of $22.2\%$ (irradiated at less than $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) shown in Fig.
Weld 7 was observed to have fracture strains at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ of 7.2 and $10.8\%$ after
The stress-rupture properties at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ of several heats of base metal are shown in Fig.
7 where a comparison is made between the stress-rupture properties of the base metal and the various welds involved in this study.
Weld 3, which involved only vacuum-melted materials, had significantly higher strength than the other welds.
Welds 7 and 8, which involved $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ and WC additions, also showed notably better performance than welds 1, 4, and 6.

The rupture lives of the welds approached those of the base metal as the stress level was reduced; this is likely due to thermal recovery of the weld metal during the long time at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The strength was improved greatly by a postweld anneal of 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The fracture strain of the base metal was about $25\%$ for short rupture lives of a few hours and decreased to values of 10 to $15\%$ when the rupture life was 1000 hr.
strains of only about $3\%$ for rupture lives up to a few hundred hours.
Samples having rupture lives over 1000 hr showed some improvement with strains in the range of 3 to $5\%$ .
Weld 3 had much superior fracture strains of about $6\%$ for all test conditions.
All welds and even the base metal exhibited much higher fracture strains after an anneal of 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

those of heat 5065 for the low irradiation temperature of less than $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , but further work has shown that these materials have comparable properties when irradiated at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ or higher. $^{6}$ The rupture lives of the welds seem quite comparable with those of the base metals and all welds seem to have about the same properties.

A single specimen was given a postweld heat treatment of 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and had superior post-irradiation strength, but this observation should be repeated before concluding that this anneal is effective.
Moteff and Smith $^{15}$ also found that the postirradiation creep properties of Hastelloy N base metal and welds were equivalent at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
They also found that a postweld anneal of 4 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ did not improve the postirradiation properties of welds.
10 as a function of rupture life.
The fracture strain of the base metal had a low value of about $0.5\%$ for rupture lives of only a few hours and increased to about $3\%$ for rupture lives of 1000 hr.
11, with heat 5065 favoring the right side and heat 2477 the left side.
12, where the data for the welds are shown for comparison.
We have mentioned previously the improvement of the properties of welds by a postweld heat treatment of 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

We chose this treatment on the basis of our work with weld 1 in which we tested samples at 40,000 psi and $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ after annealing samples for various periods of time at temperatures of 1200, 1400, and $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The as-welded sample had a rupture life of only 19 hr compared with a rupture life of about 350 hr for the base metal (Fig.
Long annealing times at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ were required to produce much improvement, and the rupture life was only 60 hr
Influence of Postweld Heat Treatments on the Rupture Life of Weld 1 Tested at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 40,000 psi.
after a 1000-hr postweld heat treatment at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Annealing at $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ brought about more rapid recovery with the rupture life of the weld being comparable with that of the base metal after a 50-hr anneal.
Only about 8 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was required to restore the rupture life.
We have no explanation for the apparent reduction in the rupture life due to a postweld anneal at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ of 50 hr compared with anneals of 8- and 100-hr duration.
Influence of Postweld Heat Treatments on the Fracture Strain of Weld 1 Tested at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 40,000 psi.
Influence of Postweld Heat Treatments on the Reduction in Area of Weld 1 Tested at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
obtained by postweld anneals at 1400 and $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The unusual behavior due to annealing for 50 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ again appears for two of the samples.
The reduction in area is not affected appreciably by annealing at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , indicating that the improved elongation at fracture (Fig.
14) is due primarily to the formation of cracks during testing.
Figure 16 shows the fusion zone of weld 4.
The structure of the base metal is characterized by stringers of $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ -type precipitates and a relatively fine grain size.
The fusion line is quite sharp and the transition of some of the large $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ precipitates to a lamellar phase is apparent.

The exact identification of the lamellar phase has eluded us for some time, but our evidence indicates that it is a Mo-Ni-Si compound.[16] Microprobe studies have shown that the precipitates are enriched in silicon and that the dark etching portions of the dendritic cast structure are also higher in silicon.
Photomicrographs of the Fusion Zone of Weld 4.
(a) Base metal on left and weld metal on right. $100 \times$ .
(b) Transformation of stringer precipitates to lamellar product at the fusion line. $500 \times$ .
This same general description applies to the fusion zones of the other welds except 3 and 8.
The base metals used in weld 3 were vacuum-melted and the stringers are minimal.
The bulk silicon content was low (0.015%) and the precipitates were primarily of the $\mathrm{Mo}_{2}\mathrm{C}$ type and were found to dissolve easily.
This sample was tested at 40,000 psi and $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and failed after 312 hr with $16.6\%$ strain.
Typical microstructures of a tested sample from weld 1 are shown in Fig.
This sample was tested at 40,000 psi and $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in the as-welded condition; failure occurred in 18.7 hr with $3.1\%$ strain and only $0.77\%$ reduction in area.
Figure 20 shows the fracture of a sample from weld 1 that was annealed for 2 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and tested at 40,000 psi and $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
This sample failed in 200 hr with $9.4\%$ strain and $6.4\%$ reduction in area.

The postirradiation fracture strains in tensile tests of the welds and the base metal became closer as the test temperature was increased; they were identical at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The postirradiation creep-rupture properties at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ were about the same for welds and base metal, even though the preirradiation properties differed greatly.
